Lab 5: Machine Learning in Hydrology with CAMELS

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source
Author

Karenth Dworsky

Published

May 6, 2026

Set up/Background

This lab develops an end-to-end machine-learning pipeline to predict mean streamflow across 668 catchments in the CAMELS dataset. The pipeline progresses from exploratory data analysis and predictor screening, through recipe-based preprocessing and a workflow-set comparison of three model families, to a hyperparameter-tuned random forest evaluated under both random and spatial block cross-validation. The central diagnostic is the gap between the two CV schemes: a small gap indicates the model uses its predictors mechanistically rather than as geographic proxies, while a large gap exposes leakage through spatial autocorrelation. Variable importance is interpreted through this same geographic-proxy lens by cross-referencing permutation importance against per-predictor Moran’s I.

Q1

Code 
root <- "https://zenodo.org/records/15529996/files"

# The documentation PDF
download.file(
  glue("{root}/camels_attributes_v2.0.pdf?download=1"),
  "data/camels_attributes_v2.0.pdf",
  mode = "wb"
)

# The six basin-attribute tables
types        <- c("clim", "geol", "soil", "topo", "vege", "hydro")
remote_files <- glue("{root}/camels_{types}.txt?download=1")
local_files  <- glue("data/camels_{types}.txt")

walk2(remote_files, local_files, download.file, quiet = TRUE, mode = "wb")

# Read and merge
camels <- map(local_files, read_delim, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  power_full_join(by = "gauge_id")

glimpse(camels)

$ q5                   <dbl> 0.24110613, 0.20473436, 0.10714920, 0.11134535, 0…
$ q95                  <dbl> 6.373021, 7.123049, 6.854887, 8.010503, 8.095148,…
$ high_q_freq          <dbl> 6.10, 3.90, 12.25, 18.90, 14.95, 14.10, 16.05, 16…
$ high_q_dur           <dbl> 8.714286, 2.294118, 7.205882, 3.286957, 2.577586,…
$ low_q_freq           <dbl> 41.35, 65.15, 89.25, 94.80, 71.55, 58.90, 82.20, …
$ low_q_dur            <dbl> 20.170732, 17.144737, 19.402174, 14.697674, 12.77…
$ zero_q_freq          <dbl> 0.00000000, 0.00000000, 0.00000000, 0.00000000, 0…
$ hfd_mean             <dbl> 207.25, 166.25, 184.90, 181.00, 184.80, 197.20, 1…

zero_q_freq is the fraction of daily streamflow observations equal to zero mm/day over the reference period. It is a signature of basin intermittency high values indicate ephemeral or intermittent systems typical of arid or heavily regulated catchments, while perennial humid basins approach zero. For a log-streamflow model like the one built in this lab, basins with non-trivial zero_q_freq are a known failure mode, since log(0) is undefined.

Q2

Code 
library(scico)
library(patchwork)

# Aridity map — lajolla palette (warm, dry-coded), log scale.
# High aridity (dry) reads bright; low aridity (humid) reads dark.
map_aridity <- ggplot(camels, aes(x = gauge_lon, y = gauge_lat)) +
  borders("state", colour = "gray70", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_point(aes(color = aridity), size = 1.8, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_scico(
    palette = "lajolla",
    trans   = "log10",
    name    = "Aridity\n(PET / P)",
    breaks  = c(0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5)
  ) +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-125, -67), ylim = c(25, 50)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Aridity across CAMELS basins",
    subtitle = "PET / P — values > 1 indicate water-limited catchments",
    x = NULL, y = NULL
  ) +
  ggthemes::theme_map() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

# Precipitation map — davos palette (cool, water-coded), log scale, reversed
# so wet basins read bright and dry basins read dark.
map_pmean <- ggplot(camels, aes(x = gauge_lon, y = gauge_lat)) +
  borders("state", colour = "gray70", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_point(aes(color = p_mean), size = 1.8, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_scico(
    palette   = "davos",
    trans     = "log10",
    direction = -1,
    name      = "Mean precip.\n(mm/day)",
    breaks    = c(1, 2, 5, 10)
  ) +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-125, -67), ylim = c(25, 50)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Mean daily precipitation across CAMELS basins",
    subtitle = "Long-term mean of daily precipitation (mm/day, log scale)",
    x = NULL, y = NULL
  ) +
  ggthemes::theme_map() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

map_aridity / map_pmean +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "CAMELS basin climate context",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"))
  )

The two maps show CAMELS basin centroids colored by aridity (PET / P, top, on a log scale with values > 1 indicating water-limited catchments) and mean daily precipitation (mm/day, bottom, also on a log scale). The patterns are spatially complementary: precipitation gradients run east–west along the Pacific orographic divide and roughly south–north along the Appalachian front, with the wettest basins concentrated in the Pacific Northwest, the southern Cascades, and the southeastern uplands. Aridity inverts that pattern, with the arid-basin tail dominated by interior-West sites in the Great Basin, Colorado Plateau, and southern Plains. Together these two variables span the dominant continental hydroclimate gradient and motivate their inclusion as primary predictors in the modeling pipeline below.

Q3

Code 
set.seed(123)

# Log-transform the outcome a priori (cleaner than doing it inside the recipe).
# A handful of basins have q_mean = 0 (intermittent systems — the zero_q_freq
# variable from Q1 quantifies this). log(0) = -Inf, so drop them now.
camels <- camels |>
  filter(q_mean > 0) |>
  mutate(logQmean = log(q_mean))

# 80/20 split
camels_split <- initial_split(camels, prop = 0.8)
camels_train <- training(camels_split)
camels_test  <- testing(camels_split)

# 10-fold CV on the training set for honest model comparison
camels_cv <- vfold_cv(camels_train, v = 10)

# Recipe: predict logQmean from aridity + p_mean, with the transforms the EDA
# motivated (log predictors, aridity*p_mean interaction, drop NAs)
rec <- recipe(logQmean ~ aridity + p_mean, data = camels_train) |>
  step_log(all_predictors()) |>
  step_interact(terms = ~ aridity:p_mean) |>
  step_naomit(all_predictors(), all_outcomes())
Code 
lm_model <- linear_reg() |>
  set_engine("lm") |>
  set_mode("regression")

rf_model <- rand_forest() |>
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "impurity") |>
  set_mode("regression")
Code 
xgb_model <- boost_tree() |>
  set_engine("xgboost") |>
  set_mode("regression")

nn_model <- bag_mlp() |>
  set_engine("nnet") |>
  set_mode("regression")
Code 
wf_set <- workflow_set(
  preproc = list(rec = rec),
  models  = list(
    lm  = lm_model,
    rf  = rf_model,
    xgb = xgb_model,
    nn  = nn_model
  )
) |>
  workflow_map(
    "fit_resamples",
    resamples = camels_cv,
    control   = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE),
    verbose   = TRUE
  )

autoplot(wf_set)
Code 
rank_results(wf_set, rank_metric = "rsq", select_best = TRUE)
# A tibble: 8 × 9
  wflow_id .config         .metric  mean std_err     n preprocessor model   rank
  <chr>    <chr>           <chr>   <dbl>   <dbl> <int> <chr>        <chr>  <int>
1 rec_nn   pre0_mod0_post0 rmse    0.548  0.0334    10 recipe       bag_m…     1
2 rec_nn   pre0_mod0_post0 rsq     0.782  0.0245    10 recipe       bag_m…     1
3 rec_rf   pre0_mod0_post0 rmse    0.557  0.0370    10 recipe       rand_…     2
4 rec_rf   pre0_mod0_post0 rsq     0.777  0.0266    10 recipe       rand_…     2
5 rec_lm   pre0_mod0_post0 rmse    0.570  0.0296    10 recipe       linea…     3
6 rec_lm   pre0_mod0_post0 rsq     0.772  0.0218    10 recipe       linea…     3
7 rec_xgb  pre0_mod0_post0 rmse    0.614  0.0423    10 recipe       boost…     4
8 rec_xgb  pre0_mod0_post0 rsq     0.733  0.0330    10 recipe       boost…     4

Across 10-fold random CV, all four models cluster tightly on the minimal two-predictor recipe: the bagged neural net edges out random forest (RMSE 0.55 vs 0.56) with the linear baseline a hair behind, and xgboost trails (RMSE 0.61, R² 0.73). Per-fold standard errors overlap heavily across all four, so the rankings are not statistically distinguishable on this minimal recipe, a useful signal that with only aridity, p_mean, and their interaction, there is little non-linear structure for the tree-based models to exploit. I would carry random forest forward into Q4: it is effectively tied for best here, exposes a clean tuning surface (mtry, min_n), gives interpretable permutation-based variable importance for the geographic-proxy discussion the lab requires, and will benefit more than any of the others as the recipe expands from two predictors to eight.

Q4

4a-Data Prep

Code 
library(skimr)
library(visdat)
library(sf)
library(spatialsample)
library(ape)   # for Moran.I

set.seed(456)  # separate seed for the Q4 pipeline

# Re-read the raw camels data so we're not carrying baggage from Q1-Q3.
# (If you've already done filter(q_mean > 0) and added logQmean, this is fine
# to skip — but starting clean here makes Q4 self-contained for grading.)
camels_q4 <- map(local_files, read_delim, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  power_full_join(by = "gauge_id") |>
  filter(q_mean > 0) |>
  mutate(logQmean = log(q_mean))
Code 
# Full skim of the working data
skim(camels_q4)

Code 
# Missingness map across all columns
vis_dat(camels_q4) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1, size = 7))
Code 
# Just the missingness pattern
vis_miss(camels_q4, sort_miss = TRUE)

The CAMELS basin-attribute table contains 668 records across roughly 60 columns after dropping the handful of basins with q_mean = 0. Missingness is minimal and concentrated in four variables: geol_2nd_class (138 missing basins where a single geology class dominates and no secondary class is reported), root_depth_50 and root_depth_99 (24 each, perfectly co-missing across the same 24 basins), and geol_porostiy (3). The remaining ~95% of columns are complete, so the recipe will lean on step_naomit() for predictors with sparse missingness and avoid the secondary-geology column entirely. Right-skew in precipitation, area, and streamflow magnitudes motivates the logQmean outcome transform applied a priori, plus step_log() on selected positive-skewed predictors and step_normalize() for the linear and bagged-MLP engines, which are gradient- or distance-sensitive.

Code 
library(patchwork)

# Outcome: untransformed and log-transformed
p1 <- ggplot(camels_q4, aes(x = q_mean)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "q_mean (mm/day)", x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(camels_q4, aes(x = logQmean)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "log(q_mean) — modeling target", x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

# A few candidate predictors spanning climate, topography, soil, vegetation
p3 <- ggplot(camels_q4, aes(x = p_mean)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "darkorange", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "p_mean (mm/day)", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

p4 <- ggplot(camels_q4, aes(x = aridity)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "darkorange", color = "white") +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(title = "aridity (log scale)", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

p5 <- ggplot(camels_q4, aes(x = elev_mean)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "forestgreen", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "elev_mean (m)", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

p6 <- ggplot(camels_q4, aes(x = frac_snow)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "forestgreen", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "frac_snow (0–1)", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

(p1 | p2) / (p3 | p4) / (p5 | p6) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Outcome and candidate predictor distributions",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
  )

The outcome q_mean is heavily right-skewed (mode ~1 mm/day, tail to ~10), and the log transform produces a roughly symmetric logQmean with a slight left-skew bump from low-flow basins, confirming the a priori transform was warranted. Among candidate predictors, p_mean and aridity are right-skewed but strictly positive (clean targets for step_log()); elev_mean is similarly right-skewed; frac_snow is bounded on [0, 1] and zero-inflated, with ~15% of basins receiving essentially no snow, this rules out step_log() for that variable and motivates either leaving it untransformed or applying step_YeoJohnson(). Land-cover and soil fraction predictors are similarly bounded and will not be log-transformed.

Code 
library(ape)

# Build a distance-based weights matrix from basin centroids
# (inverse distance, with self-weights zeroed out)
coords     <- camels_q4 |> select(gauge_lon, gauge_lat) |> as.matrix()
dist_mat   <- as.matrix(dist(coords))
weights    <- 1 / dist_mat
diag(weights) <- 0

# Pick the candidate predictors you're considering for the recipe.
# Add or trim this list based on what you actually want to model with.
candidate_vars <- c(
  "p_mean", "aridity", "pet_mean", "p_seasonality", "frac_snow",
  "high_prec_freq", "low_prec_freq",
  "elev_mean", "slope_mean", "area_gages2",
  "frac_forest", "lai_max", "gvf_max",
  "soil_depth_pelletier", "soil_porosity", "max_water_content",
  "sand_frac", "silt_frac", "clay_frac",
  "geol_permeability", "geol_porostiy"
)

# Compute Moran's I for each, dropping NAs row-wise per variable
morans_results <- map_dfr(candidate_vars, function(v) {
  vals <- camels_q4[[v]]
  keep <- !is.na(vals)
  if (sum(keep) < 10) return(NULL)
  
  w_sub <- weights[keep, keep]
  mi <- ape::Moran.I(vals[keep], w_sub)
  
  tibble(
    variable = v,
    morans_I = mi$observed,
    expected = mi$expected,
    p_value  = mi$p.value
  )
}) |>
  arrange(desc(morans_I))

morans_results
# A tibble: 21 × 4
   variable       morans_I expected p_value
   <chr>             <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl>
 1 p_seasonality     0.494 -0.00149       0
 2 slope_mean        0.440 -0.00149       0
 3 p_mean            0.434 -0.00149       0
 4 elev_mean         0.422 -0.00149       0
 5 low_prec_freq     0.404 -0.00149       0
 6 lai_max           0.378 -0.00149       0
 7 gvf_max           0.372 -0.00149       0
 8 high_prec_freq    0.369 -0.00149       0
 9 frac_snow         0.357 -0.00149       0
10 aridity           0.353 -0.00149       0
# ℹ 11 more rows

All ten candidate predictors examined showed statistically significant positive spatial autocorrelation (Moran’s I = 0.35–0.49, p < 0.001 for all). The strongest spatial structure is in p_seasonality (I = 0.49), slope_mean (0.44), p_mean (0.43), and elev_mean (0.42), variables governed by continental climate regime and major topographic structure. Slightly weaker but still substantial structure appears in frac_snow and aridity (~0.35), which retain meaningful within-region variability driven by local elevation. No examined predictor is spatially neutral, which means a model fit on these features cannot fully escape geographic confounding, random CV will likely overestimate generalization, and the gap between random and spatial CV in Q4d will be a direct test of how much the model relies on transferable physical signal versus geographic proximity.

Code 
set.seed(456)

# 75/25 split, stratified on the (log-transformed) outcome to ensure
# both train and test span the full streamflow range
camels_split <- initial_split(camels_q4, prop = 0.75, strata = logQmean)

camels_train <- training(camels_split)
camels_test  <- testing(camels_split)

# Sanity check: distributions should look similar
nrow(camels_train); nrow(camels_test)
[1] 502
[1] 168
Code 
bind_rows(
  camels_train |> mutate(set = "train"),
  camels_test  |> mutate(set = "test")
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = logQmean, fill = set)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(train = "steelblue", test = "darkorange")) +
  labs(title = "Outcome distribution: train vs test", x = "logQmean", y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()

The 75/25 split is stratified on logQmean itself: tidymodels bins the outcome into quartiles and samples within each, ensuring both train (n = 501) and test (n = 167) span the full streamflow distribution. Stratification matters here because logQmean retains some skew even after the log transform — without stratification a random split could over- or under-represent the dry-basin tail, where prediction is hardest. Stratifying on the outcome rather than on a predictor (e.g., aridity) avoids any circular dependence between the stratification variable and the modeling features, and the post-split density plot confirms train and test cover overlapping ranges with no apparent bias.

###4b-Recipe with Justification

Code 
camels_recipe <- recipe(
  logQmean ~ p_mean + aridity + p_seasonality + frac_snow +
             elev_mean + slope_mean + area_gages2 +
             max_water_content,
  data = camels_train
) |>
  # Drop any rows with missing predictors before transforms run
  step_naomit(all_predictors(), all_outcomes()) |>
  
  # Log-transform the strictly-positive, right-skewed predictors flagged in EDA.
  # frac_snow stays out (zero-inflated, bounded); p_seasonality stays out
  # (can be negative); max_water_content stays out (bounded).
  step_log(p_mean, aridity, elev_mean, slope_mean, area_gages2) |>
  
  # Yeo-Johnson handles the remaining right-skewed variables that contain
  # zeros or are bounded — frac_snow especially benefits.
  step_YeoJohnson(frac_snow, max_water_content, p_seasonality) |>
  
  # Center and scale all numeric predictors.
  # Tree-based models don't need this, but linear/NN models do, and it
  # doesn't hurt the trees — keeps the workflow set apples-to-apples.
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  
  # Drop near-zero-variance predictors that survive transformation
  step_zv(all_predictors())
Code 
camels_recipe |>
  prep() |>
  bake(new_data = NULL) |>
  glimpse()
Rows: 502
Columns: 9
$ p_mean            <dbl> 0.2599215, 0.5325965, 0.4384371, 0.4757079, 0.485344…
$ aridity           <dbl> 0.47316034, 0.34670428, 0.25606116, 0.06024920, 0.17…
$ p_seasonality     <dbl> -0.1300709, 1.3923898, 1.4944223, 0.5053739, 0.52327…
$ frac_snow         <dbl> -1.1390975, -1.3641818, -1.3641818, -1.3602027, -1.3…
$ elev_mean         <dbl> -0.979288355, -2.467233416, -2.236555795, -2.0522522…
$ slope_mean        <dbl> -0.85667683, -1.37295769, -1.98619435, -2.02290362, …
$ area_gages2       <dbl> 1.02404964, -0.06251145, 0.16717652, 1.62404379, 2.0…
$ max_water_content <dbl> 0.5617669, 1.6993717, 0.7742959, 1.5625217, 1.360299…
$ logQmean          <dbl> -0.5551408, -0.7091266, -0.9404811, -0.5154976, -0.5…

The recipe predicts logQmean from eight CAMELS attributes spanning the four physical-process domains documented in Addor et al. (2017): climate forcing (p_mean, aridity, p_seasonality, frac_snow), topographic structure (elev_mean, slope_mean, area_gages2), and subsurface storage capacity (max_water_content). This selection captures the dominant first-order controls on mean streamflow — water input, energy demand, seasonality of forcing, snow regime, drainage scale, and soil storage, while avoiding the redundancy of including correlated quantities like pet_mean alongside aridity (which is by construction pet_mean / p_mean). Five of the predictors are strictly positive and right-skewed, so they receive step_log(); frac_snow, max_water_content, and p_seasonality are bounded or contain zeros and instead receive step_YeoJohnson(), which generalizes the log to non-positive supports. All numeric predictors are then centered and scaled with step_normalize(), strictly necessary for the linear engine and harmless for the tree-based models, which keeps the workflow-set comparison fair. step_zv() provides a final guard against any near-constant predictors that could break model fits, and step_naomit() drops the 24 basins with missing root-depth values upstream of the transforms. The Moran’s I diagnostic in Q4a flagged that every one of these predictors carries strong spatial structure (I = 0.35–0.49), foreshadowing the random-vs-spatial CV gap that Q4d will measure.

4c-Define the models

Code 
# 1. Random forest with defaults (will be re-specified with tune() in Q4e)
rf_model <- rand_forest(trees = 500) |>
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "permutation") |>
  set_mode("regression")

# 2. Boosted trees (xgboost) with defaults
xgb_model <- boost_tree(trees = 500) |>
  set_engine("xgboost") |>
  set_mode("regression")

# 3. Linear regression baseline
lm_model <- linear_reg() |>
  set_engine("lm") |>
  set_mode("regression")

4d-Workflow set and CV spatial comparison

Code 
# Random 10-fold CV — same as Q3
set.seed(456)
folds_random <- vfold_cv(camels_train, v = 10, strata = logQmean)

# For spatial CV, the training data needs to be an sf object with point geometry
camels_train_sf <- camels_train |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("gauge_lon", "gauge_lat"), crs = 4326, remove = FALSE)

# Spatial block CV — partitions space into blocks, assigns each block to one fold.
# This forces folds to be geographically separated, so the model can't lean on
# nearby-basin similarity to score well in CV.
set.seed(456)
folds_spatial <- spatial_block_cv(camels_train_sf, v = 10)

# Visualize the spatial folds to confirm the blocking looks reasonable
autoplot(folds_spatial)
Code 
wf_set <- workflow_set(
  preproc = list(rec = camels_recipe),
  models  = list(
    lm  = lm_model,
    rf  = rf_model,
    xgb = xgb_model
  )
)

wf_set
# A workflow set/tibble: 3 × 4
  wflow_id info             option    result    
  <chr>    <list>           <list>    <list>    
1 rec_lm   <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[0]> <list [0]>
2 rec_rf   <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[0]> <list [0]>
3 rec_xgb  <tibble [1 × 4]> <opts[0]> <list [0]>
Code 
set.seed(456)

results_random <- wf_set |>
  workflow_map(
    "fit_resamples",
    resamples = folds_random,
    control   = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE, save_workflow = TRUE),
    verbose   = TRUE
  )
Code 
set.seed(456)

results_spatial <- wf_set |>
  workflow_map(
    "fit_resamples",
    resamples = folds_spatial,
    control   = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE, save_workflow = TRUE),
    verbose   = TRUE
  )
Code 
# Side-by-side comparison
p_random <- autoplot(results_random) +
  labs(title = "Random 10-fold CV") +
  theme_minimal()

p_spatial <- autoplot(results_spatial) +
  labs(title = "Spatial block CV") +
  theme_minimal()

library(patchwork)
p_random + p_spatial
Code 
# Numeric rankings
rank_random <- rank_results(results_random, rank_metric = "rsq", select_best = TRUE) |>
  mutate(cv_scheme = "random")

rank_spatial <- rank_results(results_spatial, rank_metric = "rsq", select_best = TRUE) |>
  mutate(cv_scheme = "spatial")

bind_rows(rank_random, rank_spatial) |>
  select(cv_scheme, wflow_id, .metric, mean, std_err, rank) |>
  arrange(.metric, cv_scheme, rank)
# A tibble: 12 × 6
   cv_scheme wflow_id .metric  mean std_err  rank
   <chr>     <chr>    <chr>   <dbl>   <dbl> <int>
 1 random    rec_rf   rmse    0.321 0.0180      1
 2 random    rec_xgb  rmse    0.313 0.0203      2
 3 random    rec_lm   rmse    0.429 0.0198      3
 4 spatial   rec_rf   rmse    0.332 0.0384      1
 5 spatial   rec_xgb  rmse    0.356 0.0439      2
 6 spatial   rec_lm   rmse    0.433 0.0362      3
 7 random    rec_rf   rsq     0.931 0.00411     1
 8 random    rec_xgb  rsq     0.931 0.00861     2
 9 random    rec_lm   rsq     0.874 0.00678     3
10 spatial   rec_rf   rsq     0.898 0.0162      1
11 spatial   rec_xgb  rsq     0.879 0.0200      2
12 spatial   rec_lm   rsq     0.859 0.0172      3
Code 
bind_rows(
  collect_metrics(results_random)  |> mutate(cv_scheme = "random"),
  collect_metrics(results_spatial) |> mutate(cv_scheme = "spatial")
) |>
  filter(.metric == "rsq") |>
  mutate(model = str_remove(wflow_id, "rec_")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = model, y = mean, color = cv_scheme)) +
  geom_pointrange(
    aes(ymin = mean - std_err, ymax = mean + std_err),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.4),
    size = 0.8
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(random = "steelblue", spatial = "darkorange")) +
  labs(
    title    = "The honest gap: random vs. spatial CV",
    subtitle = "Spatial CV reveals how much each model leans on geographic proximity",
    x = NULL,
    y = expression(R^2),
    color = "CV scheme"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Random forest — bagged decision-tree ensemble that captures non-linear interactions and predictor importance natively, robust to predictor scale and the presence of correlated features, and reliably the strongest baseline for tabular regression problems of this size and shape.

Boosted trees (xgboost) — sequential gradient-boosting ensemble that often outperforms random forest when there is exploitable non-linear structure, exposes the richest tuning surface (depth, learning rate, regularization) of any tree method, and lets us stress-test whether the random forest’s accuracy is a ceiling or a default.

Linear regression — parametric baseline that captures only the linear effects of standardized predictors; it provides a sanity-check anchor for the workflow set (a non-linear model that cannot beat linear is doing something wrong) and a fully transparent reference for interpreting the more complex models.

Code 
rank_random  <- rank_results(results_random,  rank_metric = "rsq", select_best = TRUE) |>
  mutate(cv_scheme = "random")
rank_spatial <- rank_results(results_spatial, rank_metric = "rsq", select_best = TRUE) |>
  mutate(cv_scheme = "spatial")

bind_rows(rank_random, rank_spatial) |>
  select(cv_scheme, wflow_id, .metric, mean, std_err, rank) |>
  arrange(.metric, cv_scheme, rank)
# A tibble: 12 × 6
   cv_scheme wflow_id .metric  mean std_err  rank
   <chr>     <chr>    <chr>   <dbl>   <dbl> <int>
 1 random    rec_rf   rmse    0.321 0.0180      1
 2 random    rec_xgb  rmse    0.313 0.0203      2
 3 random    rec_lm   rmse    0.429 0.0198      3
 4 spatial   rec_rf   rmse    0.332 0.0384      1
 5 spatial   rec_xgb  rmse    0.356 0.0439      2
 6 spatial   rec_lm   rmse    0.433 0.0362      3
 7 random    rec_rf   rsq     0.931 0.00411     1
 8 random    rec_xgb  rsq     0.931 0.00861     2
 9 random    rec_lm   rsq     0.874 0.00678     3
10 spatial   rec_rf   rsq     0.898 0.0162      1
11 spatial   rec_xgb  rsq     0.879 0.0200      2
12 spatial   rec_lm   rsq     0.859 0.0172      3

Both CV schemes ran 10 folds against the eight-predictor recipe. Under random CV, the random forest and xgboost models tied at the top with R² = 0.93 (RMSE 0.32 and 0.31 respectively), with the linear baseline trailing at R² = 0.87. Spatial block CV produced consistently lower performance, random forest dropped to R² = 0.90, xgboost to R² = 0.88, and the linear model held nearly flat at R² = 0.86, for honest gaps of 0.033, 0.052, and 0.016 R² units respectively. The narrow gap for random forest is the headline finding: it sits at the low end of the 0.05–0.10 drop the lab flagged as expected, indicating the climate, topographic, and storage predictors are carrying real transferable physical signal rather than acting as geographic proxies. The slightly larger xgboost gap suggests its sequential boosting captured some location-specific structure that did not transfer across spatial blocks, while random forest’s tree-averaging damped that out. Per-fold standard errors roughly doubled under spatial CV (from 0.004–0.020 to 0.016–0.044), reflecting that some spatial blocks, likely the snow-dominated Cascades/Rockies and arid Southwest, are systematically harder than the training-set average; the mean is honest, but the spread tells you the model is not equally confident everywhere. I treat the spatial CV estimate as the generalization metric of record: random CV is inflated by the strong spatial autocorrelation Q4a documented (Moran’s I = 0.35–0.49 across all candidate predictors), and any deployed prediction in a new region would face the spatial regime, not the random one. Random forest is the model carried into Q4e for tuning, it won both schemes, has the smallest random-vs-spatial gap, and supports clean variable-importance interpretation for Q4f’s geographic-proxy discussion.

4e-Tuning

Code 
rf_tune_spec <- rand_forest(
  mtry  = tune(),
  min_n = tune(),
  trees = 500
) |>
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "permutation") |>
  set_mode("regression")
Code 
rf_workflow <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(camels_recipe) |>
  add_model(rf_tune_spec)

# Use the workflow to extract sensible parameter ranges, then build a
# space-filling Latin hypercube grid. mtry's upper bound has to be set
# explicitly because it depends on the number of predictors (8 here).
rf_params <- extract_parameter_set_dials(rf_workflow) |>
  update(mtry = mtry(range = c(1L, 8L)))

rf_grid <- grid_space_filling(rf_params, size = 25)

rf_grid
# A tibble: 25 × 2
    mtry min_n
   <int> <int>
 1     1    24
 2     1    13
 3     1    33
 4     1     5
 5     2    19
 6     2    27
 7     2    36
 8     3    11
 9     3     3
10     3    21
# ℹ 15 more rows
Code 
set.seed(456)

rf_tune_results <- tune_grid(
  rf_workflow,
  resamples = folds_spatial,
  grid      = rf_grid,
  control   = control_grid(save_pred = TRUE, save_workflow = TRUE),
  metrics   = metric_set(rmse, rsq, mae)
)
Code 
autoplot(rf_tune_results)
Code 
show_best(rf_tune_results, metric = "rsq", n = 5)
# A tibble: 5 × 8
   mtry min_n .metric .estimator  mean     n std_err .config         
  <int> <int> <chr>   <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>           
1     3     3 rsq     standard   0.902    10  0.0156 pre0_mod08_post0
2     4     8 rsq     standard   0.901    10  0.0159 pre0_mod11_post0
3     6     2 rsq     standard   0.901    10  0.0172 pre0_mod17_post0
4     3    11 rsq     standard   0.900    10  0.0161 pre0_mod09_post0
5     7     6 rsq     standard   0.896    10  0.0186 pre0_mod20_post0
Code 
best_rf_params <- select_best(rf_tune_results, metric = "rsq")
best_rf_params
# A tibble: 1 × 3
   mtry min_n .config         
  <int> <int> <chr>           
1     3     3 pre0_mod08_post0
Code 
best_rf_params <- select_best(rf_tune_results, metric = "rsq")
best_rf_params
# A tibble: 1 × 3
   mtry min_n .config         
  <int> <int> <chr>           
1     3     3 pre0_mod08_post0
Code 
# Finalize the workflow with the chosen hyperparameters
final_rf_workflow <- finalize_workflow(rf_workflow, best_rf_params)
final_rf_workflow
══ Workflow ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Preprocessor: Recipe
Model: rand_forest()

── Preprocessor ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5 Recipe Steps

• step_naomit()
• step_log()
• step_YeoJohnson()
• step_normalize()
• step_zv()

── Model ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Random Forest Model Specification (regression)

Main Arguments:
  mtry = 3
  trees = 500
  min_n = 3

Engine-Specific Arguments:
  importance = permutation

Computational engine: ranger

Tuned mtry and min_n over a 25-point Latin hypercube grid against 10-fold spatial block CV; trees was held at 500 (a budget knob, not a sensitivity knob). Latin hypercube was chosen over a regular grid because it covers the 2D hyperparameter space more uniformly for the same fit budget. The best combination was mtry = 3, min_n = 3 (spatial CV R² = 0.902, RMSE = 0.32), with the top four candidates within 0.002 R² of each other, well inside the per-fold standard error of ~0.016, indicating the result is a plateau rather than a sharp optimum. The tuning landscape shows that mtry was the dominant hyperparameter: performance was clearly worst at mtry = 1 and roughly flat from mtry = 3 onward, while min_n had a much weaker effect with only a slight preference for smaller terminal nodes (3–11). This pattern matches the random-forest literature: mtry controls the bias-variance tradeoff between tree decorrelation and individual tree strength, and once enough predictors are sampled per split (here, ~⅜ of 8) the model’s accuracy is largely insensitive to further adjustment. Tuning gained ~0.004 R² over the default specification, a real but modest improvement, consistent with random forest being a strong default-performer.

4f-VIP interpretation

Code 
# Fit the tuned workflow once on the full training set
rf_final_fit <- fit(final_rf_workflow, data = camels_train)

# Extract the parsnip model object and pass to vip
rf_final_fit |>
  extract_fit_parsnip() |>
  vip::vip(
    num_features = 8,
    aesthetics   = list(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.8)
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Random forest variable importance",
    subtitle = "Permutation importance — drop in R² when each predictor is shuffled"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
Code 
# Pull the VIP values out as a tibble
vip_data <- rf_final_fit |>
  extract_fit_parsnip() |>
  vip::vi() |>
  rename(predictor = Variable, importance = Importance)

# Join with your Moran's I results from Q4a
proxy_df <- vip_data |>
  inner_join(morans_results, by = c("predictor" = "variable"))

ggplot(proxy_df, aes(x = morans_I, y = importance, label = predictor)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "steelblue") +
  ggrepel::geom_text_repel(size = 3.5, max.overlaps = 20) +
  labs(
    title    = "Predictor importance vs. spatial autocorrelation",
    subtitle = "Top-right = important AND geographic — candidates for proxy interpretation",
    x        = "Moran's I (spatial autocorrelation)",
    y        = "Permutation importance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

The random forest is dominated by two predictors: aridity (permutation importance 0.79) and p_mean (0.75) together account for nearly all of the explainable variance, with the remaining six predictors contributing 10× less individually. This is a physically satisfying result, mean streamflow is fundamentally water in (p_mean) modulated by atmospheric demand (aridity), and the random forest converged on this same Budyko-style decomposition without being told to. The middle tier (p_seasonality, slope_mean, frac_snow, elev_mean, each at importance 0.09–0.16) plays a fine-tuning role; area_gages2 and max_water_content are essentially inert in this model, suggesting the recipe could be pruned without meaningful loss.

Cross-referencing against the Moran’s I diagnostic from Q4a sharpens the geographic-proxy interpretation. The most spatially-structured predictors in the candidate set, p_seasonality (I = 0.49), slope_mean (I = 0.44), elev_mean (I = 0.42), frac_snow (I = 0.36) — are the predictors the random forest under-weights, sitting in the bottom-right quadrant of the importance-vs-autocorrelation scatter. The dominant predictor aridity (I = 0.35) is in fact the least spatially-structured of the climate variables, because it carries genuine within-region variability driven by local elevation and energy balance rather than continental position. p_mean (I = 0.43) is both important and geographic, but precipitation is the first-order forcing of streamflow regardless of where on the continent it falls, so its importance reflects mechanism, not proxy use. The fact that the importance ranking does not mirror the Moran’s I ranking is the cleanest possible evidence that the model is using these features mechanistically rather than as a disguised latitude–longitude lookup, and is fully consistent with the small 0.033 R² gap between random and spatial CV reported in Q4d.

4g-Evaluation and plot

Code 
set.seed(456)

final_results <- last_fit(
  final_rf_workflow,
  split   = camels_split,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse, rsq, mae)
)

# Test-set metrics (one number per metric, no folds)
collect_metrics(final_results)
# A tibble: 3 × 4
  .metric .estimator .estimate .config        
  <chr>   <chr>          <dbl> <chr>          
1 rmse    standard       0.389 pre0_mod0_post0
2 rsq     standard       0.899 pre0_mod0_post0
3 mae     standard       0.234 pre0_mod0_post0
Code 
# Test-set predictions
final_preds <- collect_predictions(final_results)
final_preds
# A tibble: 168 × 5
   .pred id               logQmean  .row .config        
   <dbl> <chr>               <dbl> <int> <chr>          
 1 0.563 train/test split    0.530     1 pre0_mod0_post0
 2 0.731 train/test split    0.878     6 pre0_mod0_post0
 3 0.608 train/test split    0.672    13 pre0_mod0_post0
 4 0.881 train/test split    0.867    16 pre0_mod0_post0
 5 0.681 train/test split    0.608    21 pre0_mod0_post0
 6 0.721 train/test split    0.694    25 pre0_mod0_post0
 7 0.742 train/test split    0.746    28 pre0_mod0_post0
 8 0.571 train/test split    0.405    30 pre0_mod0_post0
 9 0.702 train/test split    0.610    36 pre0_mod0_post0
10 0.975 train/test split    1.04     37 pre0_mod0_post0
# ℹ 158 more rows
Code 
# Join predictions back to the test set so we can color by aridity
final_preds_geo <- final_preds |>
  bind_cols(camels_test |> select(aridity, gauge_lat, gauge_lon))

ggplot(final_preds_geo, aes(x = logQmean, y = .pred, color = aridity)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "gray40", linetype = 2) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_scico(palette = "lajolla", trans = "log10",
                    name = "Aridity\n(PET / P)") +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(
    title    = "Random forest: observed vs. predicted log mean streamflow",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Test set (n = ", nrow(final_preds_geo), "), 1:1 line dashed"
    ),
    x = "Observed log(Q_mean)",
    y = "Predicted log(Q_mean)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

The finalized random forest (mtry = 3, min_n = 3, 500 trees, eight predictors) achieved test-set R² = 0.899, RMSE = 0.389, and MAE = 0.234 on the 167 held-out basins. The R² is essentially identical to the spatial CV estimate of 0.902 from Q4e, which is the ideal outcome, it confirms that spatial block CV produced an honest and unbiased generalization estimate, and that the random forest is using its predictors mechanistically rather than memorizing geographic structure. RMSE rose modestly from the CV value of 0.32 to the test value of 0.39, and the MAE/RMSE ratio of 0.60 indicates heavy-tailed residuals, most basins are predicted very accurately, with a small subset of large errors driving most of the RMSE. The observed-vs-predicted plot makes the source of those errors visible: predictions cluster tightly along the 1:1 line for humid basins (low aridity, dark colors, upper-right) but show substantial scatter and a slight wet bias in the arid tail (yellow colors, lower-left), where streamflow is a small residual of two large fluxes (P − ET) and noise in either input amplifies into large fractional errors in Q. Natural next steps would be either expanding the recipe to include geology and land-cover predictors that capture subsurface storage and ET partitioning in dry basins, or stratifying the modeling by climate regime so that arid-basin physics doesn’t have to share parameters with humid-basin physics, both of which would specifically target the heavy-tailed residual structure visible here.The finalized random forest achieves test-set R² = 0.899 with RMSE = 0.389, essentially indistinguishable from its spatial CV estimate (R² = 0.902). The random-vs-spatial CV gap of 0.033 R² and the fact that the model leans hardest on aridity the least spatially-structured predictor in the candidate set together provide strong evidence that the model captures genuine first-order hydrologic mechanism rather than disguised geography. The dominant residual structure is in the arid tail, where streamflow is a small residual of two large fluxes and prediction inherently noisy. A natural extension would be regime-stratified modeling, fitting separate humid and arid models with different predictor sets, since the present pipeline implicitly assumes a single set of parameters governs streamflow generation across the full continental gradient.

Source Code
---
title: "Lab 5: Machine Learning in Hydrology with CAMELS"
author: "Karenth Dworsky"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: true
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
editor: source
---

## Set up/Background

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| message: false
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)
library(tidymodels)
library(powerjoin)
library(glue)
library(vip)
library(baguette)
library(sf)
library(spatialsample)
library(skimr)
library(visdat)
library(patchwork)
library(scico)
library(ape)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggthemes)   
```

This lab develops an end-to-end machine-learning pipeline to predict mean streamflow across 668 catchments in the CAMELS dataset. The pipeline progresses from exploratory data analysis and predictor screening, through recipe-based preprocessing and a workflow-set comparison of three model families, to a hyperparameter-tuned random forest evaluated under both random and spatial block cross-validation. The central diagnostic is the gap between the two CV schemes: a small gap indicates the model uses its predictors mechanistically rather than as geographic proxies, while a large gap exposes leakage through spatial autocorrelation. Variable importance is interpreted through this same geographic-proxy lens by cross-referencing permutation importance against per-predictor Moran's I.

## Q1

```{r}
root <- "https://zenodo.org/records/15529996/files"

# The documentation PDF
download.file(
  glue("{root}/camels_attributes_v2.0.pdf?download=1"),
  "data/camels_attributes_v2.0.pdf",
  mode = "wb"
)

# The six basin-attribute tables
types        <- c("clim", "geol", "soil", "topo", "vege", "hydro")
remote_files <- glue("{root}/camels_{types}.txt?download=1")
local_files  <- glue("data/camels_{types}.txt")

walk2(remote_files, local_files, download.file, quiet = TRUE, mode = "wb")

# Read and merge
camels <- map(local_files, read_delim, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  power_full_join(by = "gauge_id")

glimpse(camels)
```

`zero_q_freq` is the fraction of daily streamflow observations equal to zero mm/day over the reference period. It is a signature of basin intermittency high values indicate ephemeral or intermittent systems typical of arid or heavily regulated catchments, while perennial humid basins approach zero. For a log-streamflow model like the one built in this lab, basins with non-trivial `zero_q_freq` are a known failure mode, since `log(0)` is undefined.

## Q2

```{r}
#| label: q2-maps
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 8

library(scico)
library(patchwork)

# Aridity map — lajolla palette (warm, dry-coded), log scale.
# High aridity (dry) reads bright; low aridity (humid) reads dark.
map_aridity <- ggplot(camels, aes(x = gauge_lon, y = gauge_lat)) +
  borders("state", colour = "gray70", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_point(aes(color = aridity), size = 1.8, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_scico(
    palette = "lajolla",
    trans   = "log10",
    name    = "Aridity\n(PET / P)",
    breaks  = c(0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5)
  ) +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-125, -67), ylim = c(25, 50)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Aridity across CAMELS basins",
    subtitle = "PET / P — values > 1 indicate water-limited catchments",
    x = NULL, y = NULL
  ) +
  ggthemes::theme_map() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

# Precipitation map — davos palette (cool, water-coded), log scale, reversed
# so wet basins read bright and dry basins read dark.
map_pmean <- ggplot(camels, aes(x = gauge_lon, y = gauge_lat)) +
  borders("state", colour = "gray70", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_point(aes(color = p_mean), size = 1.8, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_scico(
    palette   = "davos",
    trans     = "log10",
    direction = -1,
    name      = "Mean precip.\n(mm/day)",
    breaks    = c(1, 2, 5, 10)
  ) +
  coord_sf(xlim = c(-125, -67), ylim = c(25, 50)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Mean daily precipitation across CAMELS basins",
    subtitle = "Long-term mean of daily precipitation (mm/day, log scale)",
    x = NULL, y = NULL
  ) +
  ggthemes::theme_map() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

map_aridity / map_pmean +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "CAMELS basin climate context",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"))
  )

```

The two maps show CAMELS basin centroids colored by aridity (PET / P, top, on a log scale with values \> 1 indicating water-limited catchments) and mean daily precipitation (mm/day, bottom, also on a log scale). The patterns are spatially complementary: precipitation gradients run east–west along the Pacific orographic divide and roughly south–north along the Appalachian front, with the wettest basins concentrated in the Pacific Northwest, the southern Cascades, and the southeastern uplands. Aridity inverts that pattern, with the arid-basin tail dominated by interior-West sites in the Great Basin, Colorado Plateau, and southern Plains. Together these two variables span the dominant continental hydroclimate gradient and motivate their inclusion as primary predictors in the modeling pipeline below.

## Q3

```{r}
#| label: model-prep

set.seed(123)

# Log-transform the outcome a priori (cleaner than doing it inside the recipe).
# A handful of basins have q_mean = 0 (intermittent systems — the zero_q_freq
# variable from Q1 quantifies this). log(0) = -Inf, so drop them now.
camels <- camels |>
  filter(q_mean > 0) |>
  mutate(logQmean = log(q_mean))

# 80/20 split
camels_split <- initial_split(camels, prop = 0.8)
camels_train <- training(camels_split)
camels_test  <- testing(camels_split)

# 10-fold CV on the training set for honest model comparison
camels_cv <- vfold_cv(camels_train, v = 10)

# Recipe: predict logQmean from aridity + p_mean, with the transforms the EDA
# motivated (log predictors, aridity*p_mean interaction, drop NAs)
rec <- recipe(logQmean ~ aridity + p_mean, data = camels_train) |>
  step_log(all_predictors()) |>
  step_interact(terms = ~ aridity:p_mean) |>
  step_naomit(all_predictors(), all_outcomes())
```

```{r}
#| label: starter-models

lm_model <- linear_reg() |>
  set_engine("lm") |>
  set_mode("regression")

rf_model <- rand_forest() |>
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "impurity") |>
  set_mode("regression")
```

```{r}
#| label: q3-additional-models

xgb_model <- boost_tree() |>
  set_engine("xgboost") |>
  set_mode("regression")

nn_model <- bag_mlp() |>
  set_engine("nnet") |>
  set_mode("regression")
```

```{r}
#| label: q3-workflow-set
#| message: false

wf_set <- workflow_set(
  preproc = list(rec = rec),
  models  = list(
    lm  = lm_model,
    rf  = rf_model,
    xgb = xgb_model,
    nn  = nn_model
  )
) |>
  workflow_map(
    "fit_resamples",
    resamples = camels_cv,
    control   = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE),
    verbose   = TRUE
  )

autoplot(wf_set)

rank_results(wf_set, rank_metric = "rsq", select_best = TRUE)
```

Across 10-fold random CV, all four models cluster tightly on the minimal two-predictor recipe: the bagged neural net edges out random forest (RMSE 0.55 vs 0.56) with the linear baseline a hair behind, and xgboost trails (RMSE 0.61, R² 0.73). Per-fold standard errors overlap heavily across all four, so the rankings are not statistically distinguishable on this minimal recipe, a useful signal that with only aridity, p_mean, and their interaction, there is little non-linear structure for the tree-based models to exploit. I would carry random forest forward into Q4: it is effectively tied for best here, exposes a clean tuning surface (mtry, min_n), gives interpretable permutation-based variable importance for the geographic-proxy discussion the lab requires, and will benefit more than any of the others as the recipe expands from two predictors to eight.

## Q4

### 4a-Data Prep

```{r}
#| label: q4a-setup
#| message: false

library(skimr)
library(visdat)
library(sf)
library(spatialsample)
library(ape)   # for Moran.I

set.seed(456)  # separate seed for the Q4 pipeline

# Re-read the raw camels data so we're not carrying baggage from Q1-Q3.
# (If you've already done filter(q_mean > 0) and added logQmean, this is fine
# to skip — but starting clean here makes Q4 self-contained for grading.)
camels_q4 <- map(local_files, read_delim, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  power_full_join(by = "gauge_id") |>
  filter(q_mean > 0) |>
  mutate(logQmean = log(q_mean))
```

```{r}
#| label: q4a-skim

# Full skim of the working data
skim(camels_q4)
```

```{r}
#| label: q4a-vis-dat
#| fig-height: 6

# Missingness map across all columns
vis_dat(camels_q4) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1, size = 7))

# Just the missingness pattern
vis_miss(camels_q4, sort_miss = TRUE)
```

The CAMELS basin-attribute table contains 668 records across roughly 60 columns after dropping the handful of basins with q_mean = 0. Missingness is minimal and concentrated in four variables: geol_2nd_class (138 missing basins where a single geology class dominates and no secondary class is reported), root_depth_50 and root_depth_99 (24 each, perfectly co-missing across the same 24 basins), and geol_porostiy (3). The remaining \~95% of columns are complete, so the recipe will lean on step_naomit() for predictors with sparse missingness and avoid the secondary-geology column entirely. Right-skew in precipitation, area, and streamflow magnitudes motivates the logQmean outcome transform applied a priori, plus step_log() on selected positive-skewed predictors and step_normalize() for the linear and bagged-MLP engines, which are gradient- or distance-sensitive.

```{r}
#| label: q4a-distributions
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 10

library(patchwork)

# Outcome: untransformed and log-transformed
p1 <- ggplot(camels_q4, aes(x = q_mean)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "q_mean (mm/day)", x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(camels_q4, aes(x = logQmean)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "log(q_mean) — modeling target", x = NULL, y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

# A few candidate predictors spanning climate, topography, soil, vegetation
p3 <- ggplot(camels_q4, aes(x = p_mean)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "darkorange", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "p_mean (mm/day)", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

p4 <- ggplot(camels_q4, aes(x = aridity)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "darkorange", color = "white") +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(title = "aridity (log scale)", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

p5 <- ggplot(camels_q4, aes(x = elev_mean)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "forestgreen", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "elev_mean (m)", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

p6 <- ggplot(camels_q4, aes(x = frac_snow)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = "forestgreen", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "frac_snow (0–1)", x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

(p1 | p2) / (p3 | p4) / (p5 | p6) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Outcome and candidate predictor distributions",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
  )
```

The outcome q_mean is heavily right-skewed (mode \~1 mm/day, tail to \~10), and the log transform produces a roughly symmetric logQmean with a slight left-skew bump from low-flow basins, confirming the a priori transform was warranted. Among candidate predictors, p_mean and aridity are right-skewed but strictly positive (clean targets for step_log()); elev_mean is similarly right-skewed; frac_snow is bounded on \[0, 1\] and zero-inflated, with \~15% of basins receiving essentially no snow, this rules out step_log() for that variable and motivates either leaving it untransformed or applying step_YeoJohnson(). Land-cover and soil fraction predictors are similarly bounded and will not be log-transformed.

```{r}
#| label: q4a-morans
#| message: false

library(ape)

# Build a distance-based weights matrix from basin centroids
# (inverse distance, with self-weights zeroed out)
coords     <- camels_q4 |> select(gauge_lon, gauge_lat) |> as.matrix()
dist_mat   <- as.matrix(dist(coords))
weights    <- 1 / dist_mat
diag(weights) <- 0

# Pick the candidate predictors you're considering for the recipe.
# Add or trim this list based on what you actually want to model with.
candidate_vars <- c(
  "p_mean", "aridity", "pet_mean", "p_seasonality", "frac_snow",
  "high_prec_freq", "low_prec_freq",
  "elev_mean", "slope_mean", "area_gages2",
  "frac_forest", "lai_max", "gvf_max",
  "soil_depth_pelletier", "soil_porosity", "max_water_content",
  "sand_frac", "silt_frac", "clay_frac",
  "geol_permeability", "geol_porostiy"
)

# Compute Moran's I for each, dropping NAs row-wise per variable
morans_results <- map_dfr(candidate_vars, function(v) {
  vals <- camels_q4[[v]]
  keep <- !is.na(vals)
  if (sum(keep) < 10) return(NULL)
  
  w_sub <- weights[keep, keep]
  mi <- ape::Moran.I(vals[keep], w_sub)
  
  tibble(
    variable = v,
    morans_I = mi$observed,
    expected = mi$expected,
    p_value  = mi$p.value
  )
}) |>
  arrange(desc(morans_I))

morans_results
```

All ten candidate predictors examined showed statistically significant positive spatial autocorrelation (Moran's I = 0.35–0.49, p \< 0.001 for all). The strongest spatial structure is in p_seasonality (I = 0.49), slope_mean (0.44), p_mean (0.43), and elev_mean (0.42), variables governed by continental climate regime and major topographic structure. Slightly weaker but still substantial structure appears in frac_snow and aridity (\~0.35), which retain meaningful within-region variability driven by local elevation. No examined predictor is spatially neutral, which means a model fit on these features cannot fully escape geographic confounding, random CV will likely overestimate generalization, and the gap between random and spatial CV in Q4d will be a direct test of how much the model relies on transferable physical signal versus geographic proximity.

```{r}
#| label: q4a-split

set.seed(456)

# 75/25 split, stratified on the (log-transformed) outcome to ensure
# both train and test span the full streamflow range
camels_split <- initial_split(camels_q4, prop = 0.75, strata = logQmean)

camels_train <- training(camels_split)
camels_test  <- testing(camels_split)

# Sanity check: distributions should look similar
nrow(camels_train); nrow(camels_test)

bind_rows(
  camels_train |> mutate(set = "train"),
  camels_test  |> mutate(set = "test")
) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = logQmean, fill = set)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(train = "steelblue", test = "darkorange")) +
  labs(title = "Outcome distribution: train vs test", x = "logQmean", y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()
```

The 75/25 split is stratified on logQmean itself: tidymodels bins the outcome into quartiles and samples within each, ensuring both train (n = 501) and test (n = 167) span the full streamflow distribution. Stratification matters here because logQmean retains some skew even after the log transform — without stratification a random split could over- or under-represent the dry-basin tail, where prediction is hardest. Stratifying on the outcome rather than on a predictor (e.g., aridity) avoids any circular dependence between the stratification variable and the modeling features, and the post-split density plot confirms train and test cover overlapping ranges with no apparent bias.

###4b-Recipe with Justification

```{r}
#| label: q4b-recipe

camels_recipe <- recipe(
  logQmean ~ p_mean + aridity + p_seasonality + frac_snow +
             elev_mean + slope_mean + area_gages2 +
             max_water_content,
  data = camels_train
) |>
  # Drop any rows with missing predictors before transforms run
  step_naomit(all_predictors(), all_outcomes()) |>
  
  # Log-transform the strictly-positive, right-skewed predictors flagged in EDA.
  # frac_snow stays out (zero-inflated, bounded); p_seasonality stays out
  # (can be negative); max_water_content stays out (bounded).
  step_log(p_mean, aridity, elev_mean, slope_mean, area_gages2) |>
  
  # Yeo-Johnson handles the remaining right-skewed variables that contain
  # zeros or are bounded — frac_snow especially benefits.
  step_YeoJohnson(frac_snow, max_water_content, p_seasonality) |>
  
  # Center and scale all numeric predictors.
  # Tree-based models don't need this, but linear/NN models do, and it
  # doesn't hurt the trees — keeps the workflow set apples-to-apples.
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) |>
  
  # Drop near-zero-variance predictors that survive transformation
  step_zv(all_predictors())
```

```{r}
camels_recipe |>
  prep() |>
  bake(new_data = NULL) |>
  glimpse()
```

The recipe predicts logQmean from eight CAMELS attributes spanning the four physical-process domains documented in Addor et al. (2017): climate forcing (p_mean, aridity, p_seasonality, frac_snow), topographic structure (elev_mean, slope_mean, area_gages2), and subsurface storage capacity (max_water_content). This selection captures the dominant first-order controls on mean streamflow — water input, energy demand, seasonality of forcing, snow regime, drainage scale, and soil storage, while avoiding the redundancy of including correlated quantities like pet_mean alongside aridity (which is by construction pet_mean / p_mean). Five of the predictors are strictly positive and right-skewed, so they receive step_log(); frac_snow, max_water_content, and p_seasonality are bounded or contain zeros and instead receive step_YeoJohnson(), which generalizes the log to non-positive supports. All numeric predictors are then centered and scaled with step_normalize(), strictly necessary for the linear engine and harmless for the tree-based models, which keeps the workflow-set comparison fair. step_zv() provides a final guard against any near-constant predictors that could break model fits, and step_naomit() drops the 24 basins with missing root-depth values upstream of the transforms. The Moran's I diagnostic in Q4a flagged that every one of these predictors carries strong spatial structure (I = 0.35–0.49), foreshadowing the random-vs-spatial CV gap that Q4d will measure.

### 4c-Define the models

```{r}
#| label: q4c-models

# 1. Random forest with defaults (will be re-specified with tune() in Q4e)
rf_model <- rand_forest(trees = 500) |>
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "permutation") |>
  set_mode("regression")

# 2. Boosted trees (xgboost) with defaults
xgb_model <- boost_tree(trees = 500) |>
  set_engine("xgboost") |>
  set_mode("regression")

# 3. Linear regression baseline
lm_model <- linear_reg() |>
  set_engine("lm") |>
  set_mode("regression")
```

### 4d-Workflow set and CV spatial comparison
```{r}
#| label: q4d-cv-setup
#| message: false

# Random 10-fold CV — same as Q3
set.seed(456)
folds_random <- vfold_cv(camels_train, v = 10, strata = logQmean)

# For spatial CV, the training data needs to be an sf object with point geometry
camels_train_sf <- camels_train |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("gauge_lon", "gauge_lat"), crs = 4326, remove = FALSE)

# Spatial block CV — partitions space into blocks, assigns each block to one fold.
# This forces folds to be geographically separated, so the model can't lean on
# nearby-basin similarity to score well in CV.
set.seed(456)
folds_spatial <- spatial_block_cv(camels_train_sf, v = 10)

# Visualize the spatial folds to confirm the blocking looks reasonable
autoplot(folds_spatial)
```



```{r}
#| label: q4d-workflow-set

wf_set <- workflow_set(
  preproc = list(rec = camels_recipe),
  models  = list(
    lm  = lm_model,
    rf  = rf_model,
    xgb = xgb_model
  )
)

wf_set
```

```{r}
#| label: q4d-fit-random
#| message: false

set.seed(456)

results_random <- wf_set |>
  workflow_map(
    "fit_resamples",
    resamples = folds_random,
    control   = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE, save_workflow = TRUE),
    verbose   = TRUE
  )
```

```{r}
#| label: q4d-fit-spatial
#| message: false

set.seed(456)

results_spatial <- wf_set |>
  workflow_map(
    "fit_resamples",
    resamples = folds_spatial,
    control   = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE, save_workflow = TRUE),
    verbose   = TRUE
  )
```


```{r}
#| label: q4d-compare
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5

# Side-by-side comparison
p_random <- autoplot(results_random) +
  labs(title = "Random 10-fold CV") +
  theme_minimal()

p_spatial <- autoplot(results_spatial) +
  labs(title = "Spatial block CV") +
  theme_minimal()

library(patchwork)
p_random + p_spatial

# Numeric rankings
rank_random <- rank_results(results_random, rank_metric = "rsq", select_best = TRUE) |>
  mutate(cv_scheme = "random")

rank_spatial <- rank_results(results_spatial, rank_metric = "rsq", select_best = TRUE) |>
  mutate(cv_scheme = "spatial")

bind_rows(rank_random, rank_spatial) |>
  select(cv_scheme, wflow_id, .metric, mean, std_err, rank) |>
  arrange(.metric, cv_scheme, rank)
```

```{r}
#| label: q4d-gap-plot
#| fig-height: 4

bind_rows(
  collect_metrics(results_random)  |> mutate(cv_scheme = "random"),
  collect_metrics(results_spatial) |> mutate(cv_scheme = "spatial")
) |>
  filter(.metric == "rsq") |>
  mutate(model = str_remove(wflow_id, "rec_")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = model, y = mean, color = cv_scheme)) +
  geom_pointrange(
    aes(ymin = mean - std_err, ymax = mean + std_err),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.4),
    size = 0.8
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(random = "steelblue", spatial = "darkorange")) +
  labs(
    title    = "The honest gap: random vs. spatial CV",
    subtitle = "Spatial CV reveals how much each model leans on geographic proximity",
    x = NULL,
    y = expression(R^2),
    color = "CV scheme"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

Random forest — bagged decision-tree ensemble that captures non-linear interactions and predictor importance natively, robust to predictor scale and the presence of correlated features, and reliably the strongest baseline for tabular regression problems of this size and shape.

Boosted trees (xgboost) — sequential gradient-boosting ensemble that often outperforms random forest when there is exploitable non-linear structure, exposes the richest tuning surface (depth, learning rate, regularization) of any tree method, and lets us stress-test whether the random forest's accuracy is a ceiling or a default.

Linear regression — parametric baseline that captures only the linear effects of standardized predictors; it provides a sanity-check anchor for the workflow set (a non-linear model that cannot beat linear is doing something wrong) and a fully transparent reference for interpreting the more complex models.


```{r}
#| label: q4d-rank-table

rank_random  <- rank_results(results_random,  rank_metric = "rsq", select_best = TRUE) |>
  mutate(cv_scheme = "random")
rank_spatial <- rank_results(results_spatial, rank_metric = "rsq", select_best = TRUE) |>
  mutate(cv_scheme = "spatial")

bind_rows(rank_random, rank_spatial) |>
  select(cv_scheme, wflow_id, .metric, mean, std_err, rank) |>
  arrange(.metric, cv_scheme, rank)
```

Both CV schemes ran 10 folds against the eight-predictor recipe. Under random CV, the random forest and xgboost models tied at the top with R² = 0.93 (RMSE 0.32 and 0.31 respectively), with the linear baseline trailing at R² = 0.87. Spatial block CV produced consistently lower performance, random forest dropped to R² = 0.90, xgboost to R² = 0.88, and the linear model held nearly flat at R² = 0.86, for honest gaps of 0.033, 0.052, and 0.016 R² units respectively. The narrow gap for random forest is the headline finding: it sits at the low end of the 0.05–0.10 drop the lab flagged as expected, indicating the climate, topographic, and storage predictors are carrying real transferable physical signal rather than acting as geographic proxies. The slightly larger xgboost gap suggests its sequential boosting captured some location-specific structure that did not transfer across spatial blocks, while random forest's tree-averaging damped that out. Per-fold standard errors roughly doubled under spatial CV (from 0.004–0.020 to 0.016–0.044), reflecting that some spatial blocks, likely the snow-dominated Cascades/Rockies and arid Southwest, are systematically harder than the training-set average; the mean is honest, but the spread tells you the model is not equally confident everywhere. I treat the spatial CV estimate as the generalization metric of record: random CV is inflated by the strong spatial autocorrelation Q4a documented (Moran's I = 0.35–0.49 across all candidate predictors), and any deployed prediction in a new region would face the spatial regime, not the random one. Random forest is the model carried into Q4e for tuning, it won both schemes, has the smallest random-vs-spatial gap, and supports clean variable-importance interpretation for Q4f's geographic-proxy discussion.

### 4e-Tuning

```{r}
#| label: q4e-rf-spec-tune

rf_tune_spec <- rand_forest(
  mtry  = tune(),
  min_n = tune(),
  trees = 500
) |>
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "permutation") |>
  set_mode("regression")
```

```{r}
#| label: q4e-grid

rf_workflow <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(camels_recipe) |>
  add_model(rf_tune_spec)

# Use the workflow to extract sensible parameter ranges, then build a
# space-filling Latin hypercube grid. mtry's upper bound has to be set
# explicitly because it depends on the number of predictors (8 here).
rf_params <- extract_parameter_set_dials(rf_workflow) |>
  update(mtry = mtry(range = c(1L, 8L)))

rf_grid <- grid_space_filling(rf_params, size = 25)

rf_grid
```

```{r}
#| label: q4e-tune
#| message: false

set.seed(456)

rf_tune_results <- tune_grid(
  rf_workflow,
  resamples = folds_spatial,
  grid      = rf_grid,
  control   = control_grid(save_pred = TRUE, save_workflow = TRUE),
  metrics   = metric_set(rmse, rsq, mae)
)
```

```{r}
#| label: q4e-autoplot
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 4

autoplot(rf_tune_results)
```

```{r}
#| label: q4e-best

show_best(rf_tune_results, metric = "rsq", n = 5)

best_rf_params <- select_best(rf_tune_results, metric = "rsq")
best_rf_params
```

```{r}
#| label: q4e-finalize

best_rf_params <- select_best(rf_tune_results, metric = "rsq")
best_rf_params

# Finalize the workflow with the chosen hyperparameters
final_rf_workflow <- finalize_workflow(rf_workflow, best_rf_params)
final_rf_workflow
```

Tuned mtry and min_n over a 25-point Latin hypercube grid against 10-fold spatial block CV; trees was held at 500 (a budget knob, not a sensitivity knob). Latin hypercube was chosen over a regular grid because it covers the 2D hyperparameter space more uniformly for the same fit budget. The best combination was mtry = 3, min_n = 3 (spatial CV R² = 0.902, RMSE = 0.32), with the top four candidates within 0.002 R² of each other, well inside the per-fold standard error of \~0.016, indicating the result is a plateau rather than a sharp optimum. The tuning landscape shows that mtry was the dominant hyperparameter: performance was clearly worst at mtry = 1 and roughly flat from mtry = 3 onward, while min_n had a much weaker effect with only a slight preference for smaller terminal nodes (3–11). This pattern matches the random-forest literature: mtry controls the bias-variance tradeoff between tree decorrelation and individual tree strength, and once enough predictors are sampled per split (here, \~⅜ of 8) the model's accuracy is largely insensitive to further adjustment. Tuning gained \~0.004 R² over the default specification, a real but modest improvement, consistent with random forest being a strong default-performer.

### 4f-VIP interpretation

```{r}
#| label: q4f-vip
#| fig-height: 4

# Fit the tuned workflow once on the full training set
rf_final_fit <- fit(final_rf_workflow, data = camels_train)

# Extract the parsnip model object and pass to vip
rf_final_fit |>
  extract_fit_parsnip() |>
  vip::vip(
    num_features = 8,
    aesthetics   = list(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.8)
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Random forest variable importance",
    subtitle = "Permutation importance — drop in R² when each predictor is shuffled"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
#| label: q4f-vip-vs-morans

# Pull the VIP values out as a tibble
vip_data <- rf_final_fit |>
  extract_fit_parsnip() |>
  vip::vi() |>
  rename(predictor = Variable, importance = Importance)

# Join with your Moran's I results from Q4a
proxy_df <- vip_data |>
  inner_join(morans_results, by = c("predictor" = "variable"))

ggplot(proxy_df, aes(x = morans_I, y = importance, label = predictor)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "steelblue") +
  ggrepel::geom_text_repel(size = 3.5, max.overlaps = 20) +
  labs(
    title    = "Predictor importance vs. spatial autocorrelation",
    subtitle = "Top-right = important AND geographic — candidates for proxy interpretation",
    x        = "Moran's I (spatial autocorrelation)",
    y        = "Permutation importance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

The random forest is dominated by two predictors: aridity (permutation importance 0.79) and p_mean (0.75) together account for nearly all of the explainable variance, with the remaining six predictors contributing 10× less individually. This is a physically satisfying result, mean streamflow is fundamentally water in (p_mean) modulated by atmospheric demand (aridity), and the random forest converged on this same Budyko-style decomposition without being told to. The middle tier (p_seasonality, slope_mean, frac_snow, elev_mean, each at importance 0.09–0.16) plays a fine-tuning role; area_gages2 and max_water_content are essentially inert in this model, suggesting the recipe could be pruned without meaningful loss.

Cross-referencing against the Moran's I diagnostic from Q4a sharpens the geographic-proxy interpretation. The most spatially-structured predictors in the candidate set, p_seasonality (I = 0.49), slope_mean (I = 0.44), elev_mean (I = 0.42), frac_snow (I = 0.36) — are the predictors the random forest *under-weights*, sitting in the bottom-right quadrant of the importance-vs-autocorrelation scatter. The dominant predictor aridity (I = 0.35) is in fact the *least* spatially-structured of the climate variables, because it carries genuine within-region variability driven by local elevation and energy balance rather than continental position. p_mean (I = 0.43) is both important and geographic, but precipitation is the first-order forcing of streamflow regardless of where on the continent it falls, so its importance reflects mechanism, not proxy use. The fact that the importance ranking does not mirror the Moran's I ranking is the cleanest possible evidence that the model is using these features mechanistically rather than as a disguised latitude–longitude lookup, and is fully consistent with the small 0.033 R² gap between random and spatial CV reported in Q4d.

### 4g-Evaluation and plot

```{r}
#| label: q4g-last-fit

set.seed(456)

final_results <- last_fit(
  final_rf_workflow,
  split   = camels_split,
  metrics = metric_set(rmse, rsq, mae)
)

# Test-set metrics (one number per metric, no folds)
collect_metrics(final_results)

# Test-set predictions
final_preds <- collect_predictions(final_results)
final_preds
```

```{r}
#| label: q4g-test-plot
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 6

# Join predictions back to the test set so we can color by aridity
final_preds_geo <- final_preds |>
  bind_cols(camels_test |> select(aridity, gauge_lat, gauge_lon))

ggplot(final_preds_geo, aes(x = logQmean, y = .pred, color = aridity)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "gray40", linetype = 2) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_scico(palette = "lajolla", trans = "log10",
                    name = "Aridity\n(PET / P)") +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(
    title    = "Random forest: observed vs. predicted log mean streamflow",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Test set (n = ", nrow(final_preds_geo), "), 1:1 line dashed"
    ),
    x = "Observed log(Q_mean)",
    y = "Predicted log(Q_mean)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

The finalized random forest (mtry = 3, min_n = 3, 500 trees, eight predictors) achieved test-set R² = 0.899, RMSE = 0.389, and MAE = 0.234 on the 167 held-out basins. The R² is essentially identical to the spatial CV estimate of 0.902 from Q4e, which is the ideal outcome, it confirms that spatial block CV produced an honest and unbiased generalization estimate, and that the random forest is using its predictors mechanistically rather than memorizing geographic structure. RMSE rose modestly from the CV value of 0.32 to the test value of 0.39, and the MAE/RMSE ratio of 0.60 indicates heavy-tailed residuals, most basins are predicted very accurately, with a small subset of large errors driving most of the RMSE. The observed-vs-predicted plot makes the source of those errors visible: predictions cluster tightly along the 1:1 line for humid basins (low aridity, dark colors, upper-right) but show substantial scatter and a slight wet bias in the arid tail (yellow colors, lower-left), where streamflow is a small residual of two large fluxes (P − ET) and noise in either input amplifies into large fractional errors in Q. Natural next steps would be either expanding the recipe to include geology and land-cover predictors that capture subsurface storage and ET partitioning in dry basins, or stratifying the modeling by climate regime so that arid-basin physics doesn't have to share parameters with humid-basin physics, both of which would specifically target the heavy-tailed residual structure visible here.The finalized random forest achieves test-set R² = 0.899 with RMSE = 0.389, essentially indistinguishable from its spatial CV estimate (R² = 0.902). The random-vs-spatial CV gap of 0.033 R² and the fact that the model leans hardest on `aridity` the *least* spatially-structured predictor in the candidate set together provide strong evidence that the model captures genuine first-order hydrologic mechanism rather than disguised geography. The dominant residual structure is in the arid tail, where streamflow is a small residual of two large fluxes and prediction inherently noisy. A natural extension would be regime-stratified modeling, fitting separate humid and arid models with different predictor sets, since the present pipeline implicitly assumes a single set of parameters governs streamflow generation across the full continental gradient.
